reviews.author: Jingbo Xu
reviews.summary: Reviewer noted that mapping_set_folio_order_range() with max_pages=0 is harmless, but did not address the main patch content.
reviews.raw_body:
mapping_set_folio_order_range(..., 0, 0) seems harmless even when
max_pages is 0.

Anyway

Reviewed-by: Jingbo Xu \<jefflexu@linux.alibaba.com\>



-- 
Thanks,
Jingbo

reviews.author: Horst Birthelmer
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Horst Birthelmer noted that the patch does not set the folio order range for fuse inodes, but was satisfied by the author's response that it is not necessary.

Reviewer noted that enabling large folios caused issues with readahead in Linux 6.17 and requested consideration for this problem.
reviews.raw_body:
I was just about to suggest something like

mapping_set_folio_order_range(inode->i_mapping, 0,
			      ilog2(max_pages ?: 1));

but you are right, it's not a problem.

---

This was actually one of the problems I ran into when I enabled large folios in linux 6.17.
Since it would create problems for readahead.

Reviewed-By: Horst Birthelmer <hbirthelmer@ddn.com>

---
Thanks,
Horst

reviews.author: Horst Birthelmer
reviews.summary: Reviewer Horst Birthelmer questioned whether bdi strict limiting was disabled for the tests, noting that large writes ran into throttling and effectively wrote pagewise instead of as intended.
reviews.raw_body:
Hi Joanne,

just out of curiosity, did you disable bdi strict limiting for this?
In my tests esapcially the large writes run into throttling pretty
fast, so that it effectively writes pagewise, which was not the target
of the test.

Thanks,
Horst

reviews.author: Joanne Koong (author)
reviews.summary:
The author is addressing Horst's concern about performance regressions when using large folios and strictlimiting off, explaining that a commit (494d2f508883) fixed the issue by changing writeback accounting. The author also provides additional benchmark results to support their claim.

Author asked for clarification on reviewer's test environment and results, specifically whether the issue was seen with strictlimiting enabled or disabled, and what test and server were used.
reviews.raw_body:
Hi Horst,

Those results are with strictlimiting on. After commit 494d2f508883
('fuse: use default writeback accounting') [1], I didn't see any
performance regressions anymore with large folios + strictlimiting on.
More information on why that commit fixed the issue is in [2].

When I ran the benchmarks last week with strictlimiting off, I saw roughly:
    tmpfs:
      seq,  128k    1174 -> 1648 MiB/s    +40%
      seq,  1M      1261 -> 1845 MiB/s    +46%
      rand, 128k    1148 -> 1638 MiB/s    +43%
      rand, 1M      1273 -> 2065 MiB/s    +62%

    xfs on NVMe:
      seq,  128k     621 ->  740 MiB/s    +19%
      seq,  1M       649 ->  776 MiB/s    +20%
      rand, 128k    1020 -> 1515 MiB/s    +49%
      rand, 1M      1125 -> 1895 MiB/s    +68%

Strict limiting on actually had better performance here, which I think
is because with the small dirty limit, the dirtying and the writeback
happen in parallel instead of more dirty pages accumulating and then
writeback getting kicked off. Because the backing device is so fast,
it didn't cost throughput for the dirtying and the writeback to happen
concurrently. The benchmarks were run with fsync, so everything had to
be flushed before the fio run returned. If the backing device was slow
and writes were bursty and fsync wasn't enforced, I think there'd
probably be better performance with strictlimiting off than on, since
the writer would be throttled to the speed of the backing device with
strictlimtiing on.

---

Are you seeing this on your system with strictlimiting on or off? Is
that with commit 494d2f508883 in your tree? What test and server were
you running? Do you know what the speed of the backing device is?

Thanks,
Joanne

[1] https://git.kernel.org/pub/scm/linux/kernel/git/torvalds/linux.git/commit/fs/fuse/file.c?id=494d2f508883a6e5c4530e5c6b3c8b2bbfb7318d
[2] https://lore.kernel.org/linux-fsdevel/CAJnrk1ZSaNRr-HWw-hbo2=LmbZiNGZveb0MwxZbPtBDFgg2icQ@mail.gmail.com/

2026-06-24.patch_summary: This patch enables large folios for the FUSE file system by capping the maximum order at the largest request that can be issued to a fuse device, ensuring that each folio fits within a single request. This change allows the allocator to fall back to smaller folios under memory pressure. The patch includes benchmark results showing significant performance improvements in various workloads, including sequential reads and writes, with some cases seeing up to 141% increase in throughput.

reviews.author: Joanne Koong (author)
reviews.summary: Author asked for clarification on whether the issue persists after applying a separate patch that fixes writeback accounting
reviews.raw_body:
With the accounting fix applied, do you still see the issue?

Thanks,
Joanne

reviews.author: Horst Birthelmer
reviews.summary: Reviewer Horst Birthelmer pointed out that the patch to remove fuse's own writeback accounting cannot be applied because large folios are enabled, which means fuse is still using its own accounting.
reviews.raw_body:
Large folios enabled and fuse own accounting, so your patch that removes
the own accounting not applied.

2026-06-25.patch_summary: This patch enables support for large file offsets in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) kernel module, addressing an issue related to writeback accounting.
